name: Mine Hill (axinite)
short_description: Historic New Jersey mineral locality famous for exceptional axinite crystals. This flat-terrain site offers rockhounders access to specimens from the classic trap rock mining district of northern N...
description:
Mine Hill in Roxbury Township is one of New Jersey's most important mineralogical localities, renowned among collectors for high-quality axinite specimens. The site sits within the New Jersey portion of the Newark Basin, an ancient rift valley filled with Triassic sediments and interspersed with basaltic trap rock flows.

Axinite, a calcium-aluminum borosilicate mineral, crystallizes in the cavities and fissures of trap rock (diabase) that cooled slowly during the Triassic period, roughly 200 million years ago. The mineral typically forms brown, tan, or violet crystals with the characteristic sharp, wedge-shaped (axinite-like) forms that give it its name. Tourmaline and clear quartz crystals also occur here, sometimes in association with axinite.

The area was extensively mined for trap rock used in road construction and concrete aggregate during the 20th century. Collectors have worked these mines and quarries for decades, making Mine Hill a pilgrimage site for serious mineral collectors seeking New Jersey specimens. The relatively flat terrain and accessible geology make this an attractive location for rockhounders willing to search mine dumps and exposed trap rock faces.

Visitors should expect to find fragments and partial crystals more commonly than complete specimens. The best axinite examples require patience and careful observation, as crystals often hide within cavities in the dark basalt. This is quintessential East Coast rockhounding - working over mine spoil and weathered trap rock rather than digging in open ground.
history: Mine Hill sits within the Triassic-age trap rock district of New Jersey, formed when basaltic magma intruded and cooled slowly within Triassic sediments approximately 200 million years ago. Slow cooling allowed crystal formation in gas cavities and fractures, creating the axinite, tourmaline, and quartz specimens that made this locality famous among mineral collectors throughout the 20th century. Historic mining for trap rock aggregate exposed fresh mineral-bearing surfaces that attracted collectors and gave the site its enduring reputation in the mineralogical community.
tips: Search the dark trap rock (diabase) outcrops and mine dumps carefully - axinite crystals are often hidden in small cavities within the basalt and require close inspection. Bring a loupe or hand lens to identify the characteristic wedge-shaped axinite crystals (tan to brown colored) among the more abundant quartz and tourmaline. Work the slopes of exposed trap rock faces where weathering has opened cavities and fractured the dense basalt. Avoid loose rock and unstable quarry walls; focus on stable accessible ledges and talus slopes where specimens weather out naturally.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently sloping terrain typical of the Newark Basin lowlands. The site consists of exposed trap rock (diabase) outcrops, weathered ledges, and mine spoil piles. Vegetation is sparse to moderate depending on the exact area. Walking distances from parking vary by site access - expect 0 to 30 minutes on foot to reach productive collecting areas. Footing is uneven due to fractured rock fragments and rubble. Elevation approximately 400-500 feet above sea level.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through fall offers dry conditions and stable footing. Summer can be hot and humid in New Jersey lowlands, but the open terrain provides some exposure. Avoid winter months when snow and ice make the rocky terrain hazardous. Spring (May-June) and fall (September-October) offer the most comfortable collecting conditions.
written_directions: From Roxbury Township center, take Route 202 north. Look for Mine Hill Road or local trap rock quarry roads in the Roxbury-Mine Hill area. GPS coordinates (40.89893, -74.604925) pinpoint the historic mining district. Parking may be limited and access varies - some areas remain private or restricted. Check locally for current accessible collecting sites, as quarry operations and property boundaries change. The location is approximately 3-4 miles north of central Roxbury Township.
road_conditions: Paved roads (Route 202) lead to the general area. Local access roads may be unpaved and vary in condition. Quarry roads are typically rough and may be gated or restricted.
parking_notes: Parking is limited and depends on which section of the mining district is accessible. Roadside pullouts may be available on local roads. Some historic quarry areas have informal parking areas; others are private property. Confirm access before visiting.
collection_rules: Access is restricted on most private property and active mining operations. Rockhounders must obtain permission from property owners before collecting. No collecting on active quarry operations or posted private land. Respect all 'No Trespassing' signs and property boundaries. Collection amounts on permitted private property are determined by landowner agreement.
rules:
Obtain permission from property owners before accessing private land
Respect all posted 'No Trespassing' signs
Do not enter active mining quarries or operations
Keep quantities reasonable and sustainable
Fill any holes or restore the site
Leave the area as you found it
nearest_services: Roxbury Township (approximately 3-4 miles south) has gas stations, restaurants, and groceries. Chester Borough (approximately 5 miles east) offers additional services.
accessibility_notes: Uneven, rocky terrain with fractured basalt and mine spoil. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Terrain is stable once you reach the trap rock faces, but loose fragments and rubble make footing unpredictable. Not suitable for those with limited mobility. Some historic quarries have unstable high walls - stay clear of steep drops and follow safe routes along stable ledges.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at Mine Hill in New Jersey?
faq.answer: Mine Hill is famous for axinite crystals, a tan to brown calcium-aluminum borosilicate mineral that forms distinctive wedge-shaped crystals in trap rock cavities. You may also find tourmaline and clear quartz crystals associated with axinite. Complete specimens are rare, but fragments and partial crystals are more common on mine dumps and weathered exposures.

faq.question: Is axinite rockhounding near Roxbury Township legal?
faq.answer: Most of Mine Hill is private property or located within historic mining operations. Access is restricted and you must obtain explicit permission from the property owner before collecting. Always check locally and respect posted boundaries, as quarry operations and property boundaries change. Do not enter active mining quarries.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding Mine Hill axinite?
faq.answer: May through October is ideal, with peak conditions in September and October when weather is mild and footing is driest. Summer is hot and humid, while winter snow and ice make the rocky terrain hazardous. Spring (May-June) offers comfortable conditions but higher humidity typical of New Jersey lowlands.

faq.question: How far is Mine Hill from central New Jersey cities?
faq.answer: Mine Hill is located in Roxbury Township, Morris County, approximately 3-4 miles north of central Roxbury Township. It is roughly 45 miles west of New York City and 30 miles south of the New York border. Route 202 provides access to the historic trap rock mining district.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect axinite at Mine Hill?
faq.answer: Bring a loupe or hand lens to identify small axinite crystals (typically 0.5 to 2 inches) hidden in trap rock cavities. A hammer and chisel are useful for carefully extracting specimens, though many collectors work the mine dumps picking loose fragments. Sturdy hiking boots are essential due to uneven, fractured rock terrain.

faq.question: Is Mine Hill a good rockhounding site for beginners?
faq.answer: No, Mine Hill is recommended for experienced rockhounders age 12 and up. Access is restricted on private property, requiring advance permission from owners. The terrain is rocky and uneven with hazards including loose rock, abandoned quarries, and mine spoil. You need patience and knowledge of axinite crystal forms to spot specimens in trap rock, making this an intermediate to advanced site.

meta_title: Mine Hill Axinite - New Jersey Rockhounding
meta_description: Find axinite, tourmaline, and quartz at Mine Hill, a historic New Jersey mineral locality. Learn access rules, best season, and expert tips.
